timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1-7 -- Word to Zedekiah During the Siege

heb.paragraph: Zedekiah is promised he will "die peacefully" and receive a proper funeral with a royal fire (misrephот, v.5) -- the burning of spices in his honor. This conditional promise depends on submission to Babylon, which Zedekiah ultimately rejects. The promised peaceful death becomes blinding and exile (39:7).

panels.ctx: Chapter 34 records two episodes during the siege. First, a personal word to Zedekiah: surrender and live, resist and lose everything. Second, the far more significant slave-release episode -- Zedekiah orders the release of Hebrew slaves (following Deut 15), then allows the owners to re-enslave them. This reversal provokes one of the most severe judgment oracles in the book.

cross.note: The earlier word to Zedekiah: "way of life and way of death." The conditional offer persists even during the siege.
The actual fate of Zedekiah: sons killed before his eyes, then blinded -- the conditional promise forfeited.

notes.note: The message: "This city will be handed over to the king of Babylon, and he will burn it down. You will not escape but will be captured." The specificity leaves no ambiguity.
The conditional promise of a peaceful death -- possible only through surrender. Zedekiah's refusal transforms the promise into a moot point.
Calvin notes the mercy within judgment: "Even now, God offers Zedekiah a dignified end. The door of grace stays open until the last moment."
The burning of spices at royal funerals is attested in 2 Chronicles 16:14 and 21:19. It was a mark of honor, not cremation -- the body was buried, and spices burned separately.
Lundbom notes that only Lachish and Azekah remain besides Jerusalem (v.7) -- confirmed by the Lachish Letters, which mention watching for fire signals from these cities.
Brueggemann reads the conditional promise as "the persistence of divine grace under impossible conditions." Even with siege ramps against the walls, God offers an exit.

heb.paragraph: Zedekiah proclaims deror -- liberty for Hebrew slaves (v.8). The word deror is the same used in Leviticus 25:10 for the Jubilee year ("Proclaim liberty throughout the land"). But the owners recapture their freed slaves (v.11), making the deror a mockery. God responds with devastating irony: "You have not proclaimed freedom (deror) for your slaves, so I now proclaim freedom (deror) for you -- freedom to fall by sword, plague, and famine" (v.17).

panels.ctx: The slave-release reversal is the most damning social justice episode in Jeremiah. The owners freed their slaves, perhaps during a temporary lifting of the siege when the Egyptian army approached (37:5), then re-enslaved them when the crisis seemed to pass. God's response invokes the ancient covenant-cutting ceremony: "Those who have violated my covenant and have not fulfilled the terms of the covenant they made before me, I will treat like the calf they cut in two and walked between" (v.18).

cross.note: The law requiring release of Hebrew slaves in the seventh year -- the law Zedekiah briefly enforced then violated.
The covenant-cutting ceremony where God alone passes between the halved animals -- the ritual that the slave-owners violated.
"Proclaim liberty throughout the land" -- the Jubilee ideal that the slave-release was meant to fulfill.

notes.note: The sequence: Zedekiah orders release, owners comply, the siege temporarily lifts, owners recapture their freed slaves. The timing reveals the motive -- religious compliance during crisis, reversal when the pressure eases.
"You profaned my name" (hillaltem et-shemi) -- the re-enslavement is not merely social injustice but profanation of God's name. Covenant violation is blasphemy.
The devastating irony: "I proclaim freedom for you -- freedom to fall by sword, plague, and famine." God matches their action with inverted consequence.
The covenant curse enacted: those who passed between the halved calf will themselves be given to their enemies. The ritual they performed binds them to the penalty they invoked.
Calvin celebrates the divine irony: "God uses their own word against them. They proclaimed liberty and then revoked it; God proclaims a liberty they cannot revoke -- liberty to die."

The covenant-cutting ceremony (karath berith, literally "cut a covenant") involved walking between the halves of a slaughtered animal -- the implication being: "May I be cut in pieces like this animal if I break this covenant."
Lundbom calls this "the most complete example of covenant-making-and-breaking in the Hebrew Bible." The text preserves the ceremony, the violation, and the consequence in full detail.

Brueggemann reads the slave episode as "the collapse of reform under pressure." When the external threat recedes, the internal commitment evaporates. True justice requires transformation of will, not merely compliance under duress.

lit.note: The slave-release betrayal is the clearest case of covenant violation in narrative form -- ceremony performed, promise made, promise broken, curse enacted.

themes.note: Covenant and Justice both peak at 10 -- the slave-release episode is simultaneously a covenant violation and a social justice crisis.

hebtext.note: Zedekiah is promised he will "die peacefully" and receive a proper funeral with a royal fire (misrephот, v.5) -- the burning of spices in his honor. This conditional promise depends on submission to Babylon, which Zedekiah ultimately rejects. The promised peaceful death becomes blinding and exile (39:7).

Zedekiah proclaims deror -- liberty for Hebrew slaves (v.8). The word deror is the same used in Leviticus 25:10 for the Jubilee year ("Proclaim liberty throughout the land"). But the owners recapture their freed slaves (v.11), making the deror a mockery. ppl.name: Zedekiah
ppl.text: First, a personal word to Zedekiah: surrender and live, resist and lose everything…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Broken Covenant with the Slaves

translations.text: The word came to Jeremiah from the Lord after King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to proclaim freedom for the slaves.
The word that came to Jeremiah from the LORD, after King Zedekiah had made a covenant with all the people in Jerusalem to make a proclamation of liberty to them,
“ ‘Yet hear the Lord’s promise to you, Zedekiah king of Judah. This is what the Lord says concerning you: You will not die by the sword;
Yet hear the word of the LORD, O Zedekiah king of Judah! Thus says the LORD concerning you: ‘You shall not die by the sword.
